Tweet by @nurijanian (George from 🕹prodmgmt.world)
Author bio: Can I make everyone a great product manager? I will do my best | Get my AI PM OS ⬇️
Text: https://t.co/huifdsHQFS
Created: 2026-06-18T07:02:33.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":5,"reply_count":0,"like_count":45,"quote_count":0,"bookmark_count":93,"impression_count":4898}
Article content: {"cover_media":"3_2067502348872916993","plain_text":"If you're a PM who drops a PRD into Claude and asks "what do you think?", you know how this goes. The answer comes back clean and confident, and it barely helps. It calls the doc well-structured, tells you to tighten the metrics, and signs off. 
Then you walk it into the room and the staff engineer wants to know how this scales past ten thousand users, legal wants the data-retention policy, and your VP wants to know what you're not building in order to ship this. None of it showed up in the review.
A sharper prompt won't fix that.
On June 10 Anthropic shipped Claude Code v2.1.172. The new bit: sub-agents can spawn their own sub-agents, up to five levels deep, and you watch the whole tree fan out in a panel under your prompt. I read it as a signal about where this is all heading. The pattern that keeps winning is decomposition: take a hard problem, break it into subproblems, and let each one work its own piece. A single "what do you think?" is the opposite of that.
When you ask one chat to review your PRD, you're asking it to be helpful, and these models learn to agree with you. So you get an agreeable reader, and an averaged one. A real engineer is pessimistic about scale. A real exec is ruthless about opportunity cost. A real customer doesn't give a damn about your architecture. Those stances fight each other, and one assistant holding all seven at once sands them into something smooth and safe. A good review needs each of those seven at full strength, arguing with each other. Average them into one voice and you've thrown away the disagreement you needed to hear.
So that's what I run instead: seven separate reviewers, each in its own context, each reading a different framework before it says a word, each handing me questions instead of a grade.
Engineering reads a prioritisation framework, then goes after feasibility, hidden dependencies, scale, edge cases, and the maintenance bill you're quietly signing up for.
Design works the user flow and the states everyone forgets (empty, error, loading, half-finished), plus accessibility and whether you've invented a new pattern users now have to learn.
Executive reads a strategy-kernel framework (Rumelt's diagnosis, policy, action) and asks whether this ladders to company goals, what the opportunity cost is, and what the kill criteria are.
Legal flags privacy and data handling (GDPR, CCPA), retention, consent, and licensing. It opens with "this is not legal advice," which is the right amount of humility.
UX Research separates what you've validated from what you've assumed, names the assumption carrying the most risk, and points you to the cheapest study that would de-risk it.
Devil's Advocate reads a pivot-triggers framework and challenges the problem itself: are we building for three users who asked loudly, would a help doc do the job, what failed the last three times we tried this.
Customer Voice drops the jargon and talks like the person stuck using the thing: can I figure this out without a tutorial, what happens when I screw it up, why would I switch from what I already do.
Each one comes back in the same shape: severity-ranked findings with evidence, the blocking questions only, and one recommended next move. They're read-only, so they critique the doc without touching it. And they give me questions rather than verdicts, because a score doesn't improve a PRD; the questions are what send you back to the real holes.
Most PMs skip this for a boring reason. Getting seven actual people from seven disciplines into a room costs days of calendar coordination, so the doc ships on one person's read and the gaps show up later, when they're expensive to fix. Run the panel and you compress those days of scheduling into a single pass.
If you'd rather not rebuild all that by hand, it's the /review workflow in PM OS: the seven reviewers, the frameworks already wired in, the structured output, running inside Claude Code, Cursor, or Cowork. 
On a PRD it adds two more (an optimist and a critic) for nine total. It won't replace your real cross-functional review and it isn't trying to. It clears the dumb, embarrassing gaps so the room can spend its hour on the calls that are genuinely hard.
Install in Claude Code today","title":"A product org of one: how lean PMs get engineering, legal, and UX review on demand","entities":{"urls":[{"text":"https://code.claude.com/docs/en/changelog#2-1-172"},{"text":"https://prodmgmt.world/products/pm-os?utm_source=x&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=prd-review-7agents_180626"}]},"preview_text":"If you're a PM who drops a PRD into Claude and asks "what do you think?", you know how this goes. The answer comes back clean and confident, and it barely helps. It calls the doc well-structured,"}
